skill.name: building-agent-tools
skill.description: Guide for designing effective tools, APIs, and MCP interfaces for AI agents with clear names, useful context, and token-efficient responses.
skill.summary: Design agent-facing tools that are clear, consolidated, and efficient for real AI workflows.
skill.author: Byunk

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported external command, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance patterns, but review found only Markdown prose and inline identifiers. No executable code, prompt injection attempt, network access, filesystem access, or credential handling was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False positive: inline Markdown examples flagged as shell execution
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged backtick text is Markdown inline code showing tool names and schema field names, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown guidance and contains no executable Ruby, shell syntax, subprocess calls, or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: False positive: prose flagged as weak cryptography
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines discuss agent tool design, descriptions, namespacing, and response formats, with no cryptographic algorithm or crypto API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no hash functions, encryption code, credential processing, or security-sensitive cryptographic decisions on the flagged lines.

low_findings.title: False positive: design guidance flagged as system reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The flagged lines advise against poor tool design and recommend documenting parameter constraints. They do not collect system information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The content is plain guidance with no commands, API calls, environment reads, platform checks, or host enumeration behavior.

content.user_title: Design Agent Tools That Work Reliably
content.value_statement: Agent-facing tools often fail when names, parameters, or responses are unclear. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a practical checklist for better MCP tools and APIs.
actual_capabilities: Guides design of agent-facing MCP tools and APIs.
Recommends clear namespacing for related tool families.
Explains how to return useful context instead of raw data dumps.
Promotes pagination, filtering, and concise response formats.
Lists anti-patterns that make tools hard for agents to use.
Covers MCP schema basics such as names, descriptions, and input schemas.
limitations: It is guidance only and does not generate working tool code by itself.
It does not validate schemas or run integration tests.
It does not cover authentication, authorization, or deployment security in depth.
It provides general patterns, not framework-specific implementation steps.

use_cases.title: Design a new MCP server
use_cases.description: Plan tool names, parameters, and responses before implementing an MCP server.

use_cases.title: Improve an agent API
use_cases.description: Review an existing API for unclear output, missing pagination, and weak error messages.

use_cases.title: Prepare tool design standards
use_cases.description: Create shared rules for teams building tools consumed by AI agents.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Tool Idea
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to review my proposed agent tool. Check its purpose, name, parameters, expected output, and likely token cost.

prompt_templates.title: Design an MCP Tool Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a small MCP tool set for my service. Recommend namespacing, consolidated operations, parameters, and response formats.

prompt_templates.title: Improve Existing Tool Responses
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to audit these agent tool responses. Identify raw data dumps, missing context, weak errors, and places to add filtering.

prompt_templates.title: Create Agent Tool Standards
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create standards for agent-facing tools. Include naming rules, schema requirements, response contracts, pagination, and anti-patterns.

output_examples.input: Review a calendar scheduling tool design.
output: Recommendation to combine availability checks and event creation into one workflow tool.
Suggested concise and detailed response modes.
Notes on useful error messages for conflicts and missing attendees.

output_examples.input: Improve a project management MCP tool set.
output: Namespaced tool names for projects, tasks, and users.
Filtering suggestions to reduce large task lists.
Guidance to return human-readable names alongside stable identifiers.

best_practices: Start with the workflow the agent must complete, then design the tool boundary.
Return enough context for the next step without dumping unnecessary records.
Test tool outputs inside real agent conversations before publishing.
anti_patterns: Creating many narrow tools that force agents through long call chains.
Returning raw identifiers without human-readable labels or next-step context.
Leaving parameters, valid values, or error recovery paths undocumented.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps design tools, APIs, and MCP interfaces that AI agents can use effectively.

faq.question: Does it create working MCP server code?
faq.answer: No. It provides design guidance that can inform implementation in your chosen framework.

faq.question: Can I use it with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance applies to Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other agent tool consumers.

faq.question: What makes an agent tool effective?
faq.answer: Effective tools have clear names, useful parameters, concise output, and errors that guide recovery.

faq.question: Does it cover security controls?
faq.answer: Only at a design level. You still need separate review for authentication, authorization, and data handling.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it before building a tool, when reviewing a tool set, or when standardizing agent-facing APIs.
